symptoms.symptom_name: Chronic firm hyperkeratotic plantar papule with a rough verrucous surface and visible pinpoint dark specks

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A rough, hyperkeratotic, months-old plantar papule with tiny dark specks (thrombosed capillaries) is the characteristic appearance of a verruca.
A plain callus is smooth-topped with preserved skin lines and no dark specks; the verrucous surface and dark specks point away from a simple callus.

symptoms.symptom_name: Sharp pain only on direct vertical weight-bearing pressure, completely absent at rest

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pain provoked purely by vertical load and absent at rest reflects a hard lesion transmitting body weight onto the dermis (mechanical), typical of a plantar wart or corn.
A deep infection or abscess typically causes throbbing pain that persists at rest; pain confined to weight-bearing argues against an active infective collection.

symptoms.symptom_name: Disruption of the normal dermatoglyphic skin lines across the lesion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Interruption of the skin lines across the lesion (versus a callus, which preserves them) is a classic distinguishing sign of a verruca — a close-inspection finding, sealed from telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pinpoint thrombosed capillaries ('black dots') revealed on superficial paring of the hyperkeratotic surface

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Thrombosed capillaries exposed by paring are pathognomonic of a viral wart and confirm it over a callus — but revealing them requires bedside scalpel paring, which telehealth cannot perform.
Paring a callus reveals homogeneous keratin without vascular black dots; their presence excludes a simple callus.

symptoms.symptom_name: Greater pain on direct vertical pressure than on side-to-side squeeze of the lesion (pinch/pressure test)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The pattern of pain elicited by a clinician's directed pressure testing helps localise a mechanically-loaded plantar lesion — a hands-on manoeuvre, sealed from telehealth; the AI Doctor infers mechanical pain from the patient's report, not from an examination.

symptoms.symptom_name: No surrounding cellulitic erythema, purulent discharge, fluctuance or localised heat

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of hot, tender, indurated or fluctuant tissue with no pus makes a deep-space infection or abscess unlikely — a clinician-palpated finding, sealed; the AI Doctor infers this from the patient's report of no redness, heat, swelling or pus.

symptoms.symptom_name: Intact protective sensation across the foot with no diabetes or vascular disease (low-risk foot)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Neuropathic ulcers occur in insensate, at-risk feet and are typically painless; intact painful sensation and no diabetes argue strongly against an at-risk-foot ulcer and place this in the low-risk-foot category.

symptoms.symptom_name: Afebrile and systemically well, hobbling on the outer edge of the foot (mechanical functional impairment)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Antalgic weight-shifting with no fever or systemic upset indicates mechanical pain rather than a systemic infective process.

symptoms.symptom_name: Clinician-measured vitals — BP 125/82, HR 88, Temp 36.7, RR 16, SpO2 99%

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal, afebrile vitals are reassuring and consistent with a benign local problem. Sealed clinician measurement — the AI Doctor infers the patient is systemically well from the patient's own report, not a measured value.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: A chronic rough hyperkeratotic plantar papule with dark specks, mechanical pressure-only pain, disrupted skin lines and thrombosed capillaries on paring together confirm a verruca and distinguish it from a plain callus.

Pain confined to weight-bearing, no cellulitic erythema/heat/fluctuance/pus, and an afebrile systemically well patient together make a deep plantar infection unlikely, supporting reassurance plus a safety-net rather than antibiotics or escalation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Diagnostic superficial paring and/or dermoscopy of the lesion at GP or podiatry review, if the diagnosis is uncertain or the lesion is not responding
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Paring reveals thrombosed capillaries and disrupted skin lines that confirm a verruca over a callus, and enables professional debridement; not obtainable via telehealth and not required to begin empiric over-the-counter treatment for a characteristic lesion.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Blood glucose / diabetes and peripheral-neuropathy screen, if any at-risk-foot risk factors are present
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Identifies an at-risk (diabetic / neuropathic) foot, which would change the pathway from routine wart care to prompt high-risk-foot review; targeted, not blanket, testing.
Not indicated now with no erythema, heat, fluctuance, pus or fever; would only apply if a secondary bacterial infection develops.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Foot X-ray / ultrasound for a retained foreign body
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not indicated for a months-old hyperkeratotic lesion with no penetrating history; imaging would only apply if a foreign body were genuinely suspected.
